blocks.x: Squaring a side gives the area, so \(5^2 = 25\). This lesson flips the question. Given that a panel uses exactly 36 tiles in a square, how long was one edge? That backward move, recovering the side from the area, is the square root.
A square concrete pad covers \(196\) square feet. Find the length of one edge in feet.
hints: Squaring a side gives the area, so to get the side back you undo the square. You are hunting for the number that, times itself, lands exactly on \(196\).
The two landmarks \(13 \times 13 = 169\) and \(15 \times 15 = 225\) trap the answer between \(13\) and \(15\). Only one whole number lives in that gap, so test it by multiplying it by itself.
blocks.sol: The area of a square is the side length times itself, so finding the side means undoing that square. We want the whole number whose square is \(196\), which is exactly \(\sqrt{196}\). The landmarks pin it down. We know \(13^2 = 169\), which falls short of \(196\), and \(15^2 = 225\), which overshoots it, so the side must be the one whole number squeezed between \(13\) and \(15\). That number is \(14\). Checking it, \(14 \times 14 = 196\), which matches the area exactly. So \(\sqrt{196} = 14\), and one edge of the pad is \(\boxed{14}\) feet long.

blocks.x: Square root. The square root of a number \(n\), written \(\sqrt{n}\) with the radical symbol drawn as a hook and a bar over the \(n\), is the number whose square is \(n\). Squaring multiplies a number by itself, and the square root reverses that, carrying you from the result back to the number you started with. So \(\sqrt{196} = 14\) precisely because \(14^2 = 196\). When the area of a square is a perfect square, its square root is exactly the length of one side.

blocks.cap: Squaring and the square root are opposite trips along one road. Squaring carries a number out to its square, like \(7\) to \(49\), and the square root brings that square back to where it started, \(\sqrt{49}=7\). On a perfect square the return trip always lands on a whole number.

blocks.x: Perfect square. A perfect square is a whole number you get by multiplying some whole number by itself, the way \(81 = 9^2\) and \(144 = 12^2\). Taking the square root of a perfect square just asks which number was multiplied by itself to make it, and that number always comes out a clean whole number. Once you know the squares of the small whole numbers, roughly \(1\) through \(20\), reading off a root is mostly recognizing a square you have already met and naming the number hiding underneath.
A video wall is built from \(625\) identical square panels in a perfect square. Since \(20^2 = 400\) and \(30^2 = 900\), the side length is in the twenties. What is \(\sqrt{625}\)?
hints: The side length is a number in the twenties whose square is \(625\). Try squaring a value right in the middle of that range and see how close you land.
Test \(25\). Compute \(25 \times 25\). If it equals \(625\) exactly, then \(25\) is the side length, since the square root recovers the side of a square from its area.
blocks.sol: The square root of \(625\) is the nonnegative number whose square is \(625\). The landmark already narrows it down, because \(20^2 = 400\) is too small and \(30^2 = 900\) is too big, so the answer sits in the twenties. Trying the middle of that range, \(25 \times 25 = 625\) exactly. So one full side of the video wall is \(25\) panels long, and \(\sqrt{625} = 25\). \(\boxed{25}\)

blocks.x: Both \(10\) and \(-10\) square to \(100\), so two numbers satisfy \(x^2 = 100\). But the radical \(\sqrt{100}\) names only one of them, the nonnegative one. Evaluate \(\sqrt{100}\).
hints: The radical does not mean "any number that squares to 100". It means the one nonnegative number whose square is 100. Of the two candidates 10 and \(-10\), only one is zero or positive.
Ask which value is nonnegative. Since \(10^2 = 100\) and 10 is positive, the radical names 10, not \(-10\).
blocks.sol: Two numbers square to 100, namely 10 and \(-10\), since \(10^2 = 100\) and \((-10)^2 = 100\). The radical symbol is defined to name only the nonnegative one, the value that is zero or positive. Between 10 and \(-10\), the nonnegative choice is 10. So \(\sqrt{100} = 10\), never \(-10\). The symbol points to exactly one value, and that value is \(\sqrt{100} = \boxed{10}\).

blocks.x: Principal square root. A number and its negative square to the same positive result, since \(10^2 = 100\) and \((-10)^2 = 100\), so \(\sqrt{100}\) would point at two values if we let it mean either one. By convention the radical names only the nonnegative value, called the principal square root, so \(\sqrt{100} = 10\) and never \(-10\). That single rule turns the square root into a clean machine: feed it a perfect square and it hands back exactly one answer every time, and that answer is never negative.

The radical \(\sqrt{36}\) names a single value, \(6\), the nonnegative side of a square with area \(36\), and there is nothing ambiguous about where it points. But a second question wears almost the same clothes. Instead of naming a value, \(x^2 = 36\) sets up an equation and asks for every number whose square lands on \(36\). Same number, two questions, two different shapes of answer. The trick from here is reading carefully enough to tell which one you have been handed.
The equation \(x^2 = 49\) asks for every number whose square is \(49\). Since \(7^2 = 49\) and \((-7)^2 = 49\), both \(7\) and \(-7\) work. How many values of \(x\) satisfy \(x^2 = 49\)?
hints: Squaring a negative number gives a positive result, so do not stop at the first number you find. Try both a positive and a negative candidate and square each one.
Check \(7^2\) and check \((-7)^2\). If both land on \(49\), then count how many separate values of \(x\) you have found.
blocks.sol: We want every value of \(x\) with \(x^2 = 49\). Testing the obvious one, \(7^2 = 49\), so \(x = 7\) works. Now think about negatives. Since a negative times a negative is positive, \((-7)^2 = (-7)\cdot(-7) = 49\), so \(x = -7\) works too. No other whole number squares to \(49\), because anything bigger than \(7\) overshoots and anything between gives a different result. That leaves exactly two values, \(7\) and \(-7\). Notice this is more than \(\sqrt{49}\) gives on its own, since the radical reports only the nonnegative \(7\). The equation, by contrast, accepts both. The count of solutions is \(\boxed{2}\).

blocks.x: Equation vs. radical. When \(n\) is positive, asking which numbers satisfy \(x^2 = n\) has two answers, a positive one and its negative twin, because squaring throws away the sign and both land in the same place. But \(\sqrt{n}\) names only the nonnegative one of the two. So solving \(x^2 = 36\) gives \(6\) and \(-6\), while evaluating \(\sqrt{36}\) gives just \(6\). The equation can hand you two values; the radical always names exactly one. Extra context, like knowing \(x\) is negative, picks which of the two you want.

blocks.cap: The same number line, read two ways. The equation \(x^2 = 36\) lights up both \(-6\) and \(+6\), since squaring either one gives \(36\). The radical \(\sqrt{36}\) is defined to name only the nonnegative root, so it points at the single value \(6\).

blocks.x: A depth sensor satisfies \(x^2 = 324\). Two values work: \(x = 18\) and \(x = -18\). Since depth is negative, which value is \(x\)?
hints: Find the two numbers whose square is \(324\). Take the square root of \(324\) to get the size, then remember the negative version squares to \(324\) as well.
The size of \(x\) is \(\sqrt{324} = 18\), so \(x\) is \(18\) or \(-18\). Since \(x\) measures a depth below the surface, it must be negative, which rules out \(18\).
blocks.sol: Start with the equation \(x^2 = 324\). To find how big \(x\) is, take the square root of \(324\). Since \(18^2 = 324\), we get \(\sqrt{324} = 18\), so \(x\) has size \(18\). Squaring hides the sign, because both \(18^2 = 324\) and \((-18)^2 = 324\), so the equation alone leaves two candidates, \(x = 18\) and \(x = -18\). Now use the story to choose. The value \(x\) records a depth below the surface, which means \(x\) must be negative, so \(18\) cannot be the answer. That leaves the negative candidate, and a quick check confirms \((-18)^2 = 324\). So \(x = \boxed{-18}\).

blocks.x: Squaring any real number gives a result that is zero or positive. So how many real values of \(x\) satisfy \(x^2 = -9\)?
hints: Try a few numbers. \(3^2 = 9\), \((-3)^2 = 9\), \(0^2 = 0\). Every square you get is \(0\) or positive. Can any of them ever equal \(-9\)?
Since squaring any real number gives a result of \(0\) or more, no real number can square to a negative like \(-9\). Count how many real \(x\) work, and that count is the answer.
blocks.sol: Square any real number and check the sign. A positive number squared is positive, a negative number squared is positive, and \(0^2 = 0\). That covers every real number, so a square is always \(0\) or greater. The equation \(x^2 = -9\) wants a square that equals \(-9\), which sits below zero. Nothing real can reach down there, so no real value of \(x\) satisfies it. The count of real solutions is \(\boxed{0}\).

blocks.x: No real root of a negative. Squaring any real number, positive or negative or zero, gives a result that is zero or positive, never negative, since a negative times a negative flips back to positive and a positive times a positive stays positive. So nothing you put in for \(x\) makes \(x^2\) negative, which means a negative number has no real square root and an equation like \(x^2 = -25\) has no real solution. This is not a gap in your tools, it is just how squaring works: every square points the same way, away from the negatives.
Evaluate \(\sqrt{(-12)^2}\). Square first, then take the root. What whole number do you get?
hints: Follow the order of operations and square first. What is \((-12)^2\)? Remember that a negative times a negative gives a positive result, so the inside becomes a positive number before you ever touch the radical.

Once the inside is \(144\), you just need \(\sqrt{144}\). Find the nonnegative number whose square is \(144\). The radical always reports the nonnegative root, so the sign you started with does not survive.
blocks.sol: Work from the inside out. The square happens first, so compute \((-12)^2\). A negative times a negative is positive, so \((-12)^2 = 144\), a positive number. Now the radical takes over, and it always returns the nonnegative root, so \(\sqrt{144} = 12\) since \(12^2 = 144\). Notice what happened. We started with \(-12\), but squaring threw the sign away, and the radical handed back the nonnegative version. That is why \(\sqrt{(-12)^2}\) comes out positive even though the number inside began negative. The value is \(\boxed{12}\).

blocks.x: Cancellation rule. Squaring and taking a square root are opposite moves, so doing one then the other lands you back where you started: \(\left(\sqrt{n}\right)^2 = n\) and \(\sqrt{n^2} = n\) whenever \(n\) is nonnegative. The one thing to watch is that the radical only hands back a nonnegative value, so when you square a negative number first, the square erases its sign and the root can only return the positive twin. That is why \(\sqrt{(-12)^2} = 12\) and not \(-12\): the squaring wiped the sign away and the root had no way to put it back.
A square garden covers \(1{,}296\) sq ft. Notice \(1{,}296 = 16 \times 81\). Use \(\sqrt{1{,}296} = \sqrt{16} \times \sqrt{81}\) to find the side length.
hints: Split the work using \(1{,}296 = 16 \times 81\). You can take the root of each piece on its own, since \(\sqrt{16 \times 81} = \sqrt{16} \times \sqrt{81}\).
Find each small root, then multiply them. \(\sqrt{16} = 4\) because \(4^2 = 16\), and \(\sqrt{81} = 9\) because \(9^2 = 81\). Now compute \(4 \times 9\).
blocks.sol: Break the big number into perfect-square factors. Since \(1{,}296 = 16 \times 81\), the root splits into a product of roots, so \(\sqrt{1{,}296} = \sqrt{16} \times \sqrt{81}\). Each piece is easy, with \(\sqrt{16} = 4\) and \(\sqrt{81} = 9\). Multiplying gives \(4 \times 9 = 36\). To be sure, check that \(36^2 = 36 \times 36 = 1{,}296\), which matches the area, so the side length is exactly \(\sqrt{1{,}296} = \boxed{36}\) feet.

blocks.cap: A big perfect square does not have to be rooted all at once. Split \(1296\) into the perfect-square factors \(16\) and \(81\), root each piece on its own to get \(4\) and \(9\), then multiply those roots to land on \(\sqrt{1296} = 4 \times 9 = 36\). The two little grids show why, a side of \(4\) builds an area of \(16\) and a side of \(9\) builds an area of \(81\), so a side of \(4 \times 9 = 36\) builds the full area \(1296\). And the check holds, \(36 \times 36 = 1296\).

blocks.x: Product rule for roots. \(\sqrt{ab} = \sqrt{a} \times \sqrt{b}\) for nonnegative \(a\) and \(b\). When a perfect square is too big to recognize on sight, split it into a product of two smaller perfect squares you already know, root each piece, then multiply, so \(\sqrt{1{,}296} = \sqrt{16} \times \sqrt{81} = 4 \times 9 = 36\). This works because rooting and multiplying cooperate, but only when both pieces really are perfect squares, since that is what keeps every root a clean whole number.
A classmate claims \(\sqrt{1024} = 34\). But \(34^2 = 1156 > 1024\), and a number ending in 4 squares to something ending in 6, not 4. What is the correct value of \(\sqrt{1024}\)?
hints: The size test says the root sits below 34, and the last-digit test says it cannot end in 4. So try a slightly smaller whole number and square it to see if you hit \(1{,}024\) on the nose.
You want a number whose square ends in 4. A number ending in 2 works, since \(2 \times 2 = 4\). Check \(32^2\).
blocks.sol: Both tests already narrow things down before any real arithmetic. The size test puts the root below 34, since \(34^2 = 1{,}156\) overshoots \(1{,}024\). The last-digit test rules out any root ending in 4, since those square to a number ending in 6, while \(1{,}024\) ends in 4. A root ending in 2 fits the last digit, because \(2 \times 2 = 4\). The candidate just under 34 that ends in 2 is 32. Squaring it gives \(32^2 = 32 \times 32 = 1{,}024\), an exact hit, so \(1{,}024\) is a perfect square and its root is the nonnegative number that squares to it. Therefore \(\sqrt{1024} = \boxed{32}\).

blocks.x: The word radical comes from Latin radix (root), the same as the vegetable radish. Rudolff used \(\sqrt{\phantom{n}}\) in 1525. The bar on top, the vinculum, marks what sits under the root.
A square herb bed has a side of \(15\) feet, so \(\sqrt{x} = 15\) where \(x\) is the area. Square both sides to free \(x\). What is \(x\)?
hints: The variable is stuck under the radical sign. Whatever you do to one side of an equation you must do to the other, so apply the same operation to both sides that will cancel the square root.
Squaring undoes a square root. The left side \((\sqrt{x})^2\) collapses back to just \(x\), and the right side becomes \(15^2\). Now just compute \(15^2\).
blocks.sol: The equation is \(\sqrt{x} = 15\), and \(x\) is hiding under the radical. To free it, undo the square root by squaring, and to keep the equation balanced you square both sides. $$\left(\sqrt{x}\right)^2 = 15^2$$ Squaring a square root cancels it, so the left side becomes just \(x\). On the right, \(15^2 = 15 \times 15 = 225\). That gives \(x = 225\). To be sure, plug it back in. The square root of \(225\) really is \(15\), since \(15 \times 15 = 225\), so the side length checks out and the area is \(225\) square feet. \(\boxed{225}\)

blocks.x: Solving \(\sqrt{x} = k\). When the unknown is trapped under a radical, free it by squaring both sides, since squaring is the exact move that undoes a square root: \((\sqrt{x})^2 = k^2\), giving \(x = k^2\). This only works when \(k \ge 0\), because the radical names a value that is zero or positive and can never equal a negative number. If an equation sets \(\sqrt{x}\) equal to something negative, it has no solution, even when the algebra looks like it should go through.
Solve \(\sqrt{3x + 4} = 11\). Square both sides, then solve the resulting linear equation. What is \(x\)?
hints: Squaring is the move that undoes a square root. If \(\sqrt{3x + 4} = 11\), then squaring both sides gives \(3x + 4 = 11^2\). Work out \(11^2\) first.
Now \(3x + 4 = 121\) is just a linear equation like the ones from Chapter 6. Subtract \(4\) from both sides, then divide by \(3\).
blocks.sol: Start with \(\sqrt{3x + 4} = 11\). The right side is positive, so a real solution can exist. The square root and the square undo each other, so squaring both sides frees the inside. That gives \(3x + 4 = 11^2 = 121\). Now it is a plain linear equation. Subtract \(4\) from both sides to get \(3x = 117\), then divide by \(3\) to get \(x = 39\). Check it by substituting back. Inside the radical you get \(3 \times 39 + 4 = 117 + 4 = 121\), and \(\sqrt{121} = 11\), which matches. So the value of \(x\) is \(\boxed{39}\).

blocks.x: How many solutions does \(\sqrt{x - 2} = -4\) have? The radical is always nonnegative, can it ever equal \(-4\)?
hints: The radical \(\sqrt{\phantom{x}}\) always means the nonnegative root. So whatever \(x - 2\) turns out to be, can \(\sqrt{x - 2}\) ever come out negative?

Try the tempting answer anyway. Squaring gives \(x = 18\), and then \(\sqrt{18 - 2} = \sqrt{16} = 4\), not \(-4\). So even that candidate fails the check. If the one value squaring hands you does not work, count how many values are left.
blocks.sol: The radical \(\sqrt{\phantom{x}}\) stands for the principal square root, which is always zero or positive. The right side of the equation is \(-4\), a negative number, and a principal square root can never equal a negative number. So before doing any algebra you can already suspect that nothing works. Let us confirm it. Squaring both sides gives \(x - 2 = 16\), so \(x = 18\). Now check that candidate in the original equation. \(\sqrt{18 - 2} = \sqrt{16} = 4\), and \(4\) is not \(-4\). The value squaring produced does not satisfy the equation, because squaring can erase a sign and sneak in a false answer. With that lone candidate rejected, no value of \(x\) is left that makes the equation true. The number of solutions is \(\boxed{0}\).

review.x: A square garden bed covers \(121\) sq ft. What is the side length, in feet? Evaluate \(\sqrt{121}\).
hints: A square root asks which nonnegative number, times itself, gives the number inside. Here you want the number whose square is \(121\).
Try a number in the middle of your tables. Since \(10^2 = 100\) and \(12^2 = 144\), the answer sits between them, and you are told \(11 \times 11 = 121\).
review.sol: The square root of a number is the nonnegative value that squares to give it. We are looking for the number whose square is \(121\). Testing nearby whole numbers, \(10^2 = 100\) is too small and \(12^2 = 144\) is too big, so the root is between \(10\) and \(12\). Checking \(11\), we get \(11 \times 11 = 121\), which matches exactly. So \(\sqrt{121} = 11\), and that is the side length of the garden bed in feet. The answer is \(\boxed{11}\).

review.x: A square rug covers \(256\) sq ft. What is its side length in feet?
hints: The area of a square is its side length times itself, so you are looking for a number whose square is \(256\). In other words, find \(\sqrt{256}\).
Try squaring numbers near the answer. Since \(15^2 = 225\) is too small and \(17^2 = 289\) is too big, the side length sits right between them.
review.sol: The area of a square equals its side length times itself, so the side length is the square root of the area. That means I need \(\sqrt{256}\), the nonnegative number whose square is \(256\). Testing nearby whole numbers, \(15^2 = 225\) is too small and \(17^2 = 289\) is too large, so the root must be \(16\). Checking, \(16 \times 16 = 256\), which matches the area exactly. The side length of the rug is \(\boxed{16}\) feet.

review.x: A square patio covers \(169\) sq ft. Since \(10^2 = 100\) and \(20^2 = 400\), the side length is between \(10\) and \(20\). Find \(\sqrt{169}\).
hints: The answer is a whole number between \(10\) and \(20\), since \(10^2 = 100\) and \(20^2 = 400\). Try squaring a few numbers in that range until one lands on \(169\).
The number ends in \(9\), and a whole number squared ends in \(9\) only when it ends in \(3\) or \(7\). Test \(13\) by computing \(13 \times 13\).
review.sol: Taking a square root asks for the nonnegative number whose square is \(169\), the same as recovering the side of a square from its area. The landmarks pin it down fast. Since \(10^2 = 100\) and \(20^2 = 400\), the root is between \(10\) and \(20\), so I only need to search that gap. Because \(169\) ends in a \(9\), the root must end in \(3\) or \(7\). Checking the smaller option, \(13 \times 13 = 169\), which matches exactly. So \(\sqrt{169} = 13\), meaning each side of the patio is \(13\) feet long. \(\boxed{13}\)

review.x: When you write \(x^2 = 9\), both \(3\) and \(-3\) work. But \(\sqrt{9}\) names only the nonnegative one. Evaluate \(\sqrt{9}\).
hints: The radical never gives back a negative value. You only need the nonnegative number whose square is \(9\).
Ask yourself which nonnegative whole number times itself equals \(9\). Since \(3 \times 3 = 9\), that is your value.
review.sol: The equation \(x^2 = 9\) has two solutions, \(3\) and \(-3\), because each of them squares to \(9\). The radical \(\sqrt{9}\) is different from that equation. By definition it names only the nonnegative number whose square is \(9\), so it picks out exactly one value and ignores the negative one. Since \(3 \times 3 = 9\) and \(3\) is nonnegative, we get \(\sqrt{9} = 3\). So the value is \(\boxed{3}\).

review.x: A square garden covers \(400\) sq ft. What is \(\sqrt{400}\)?
hints: The square root of a perfect square is the nonnegative number that, when multiplied by itself, gives that number. Ask yourself which whole number times itself lands on \(400\).
Break \(400\) into perfect-square pieces. Notice that \(400 = 4 \times 100\), and you already know \(\sqrt{4} = 2\) and \(\sqrt{100} = 10\), so the root is \(2 \times 10\).
review.sol: I want the nonnegative number whose square is \(400\). One clean way is to split \(400\) into perfect-square factors, since \(400 = 4 \times 100\). The root of a product of perfect squares is the product of their roots, so \(\sqrt{400} = \sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{100} = 2 \times 10 = 20\). I can check this by squaring back, and \(20^2 = 20 \times 20 = 400\), which matches the area. So the side of the garden is \(\sqrt{400} = \boxed{20}\) feet.

review.x: Both \(9\) and \(-9\) square to \(81\), since \(9^2 = 81\) and \((-9)^2 = 81\). How many values of \(x\) satisfy \(x^2 = 81\)?
hints: A value of \(x\) works if \(x^2\) lands exactly on \(81\). Try the positive root, then check whether its negative also works.
Test \(9\), since \(9^2 = 81\), good. Now test \(-9\), since \((-9)^2 = 81\), also good. Count how many different numbers you found.
review.sol: We want every number \(x\) whose square is \(81\). Start with the obvious one. Since \(9^2 = 81\), the value \(x = 9\) works. Now remember that a negative times a negative is positive, so squaring a negative number gives a positive result. That means \((-9)^2 = (-9)\times(-9) = 81\), so \(x = -9\) works too. No other number squares to \(81\), because any number larger than \(9\) in size has a square bigger than \(81\), and any number smaller in size has a square smaller than \(81\). So exactly two values, \(9\) and \(-9\), satisfy the equation. This is the heart of the two-answers idea. Solving \(x^2 = 81\) gives a pair, while evaluating the radical \(\sqrt{81}\) gives just the one nonnegative value \(9\). The count of solutions here is \(\boxed{2}\).

review.x: The equation \(x^2 = 225\) has two solutions, \(15\) and \(-15\). Engineers know \(x\) is negative. What is \(x\)?
hints: The principal square root \(\sqrt{225}\) gives you the positive number whose square is \(225\). Find that whole number first.
Once you know \(\sqrt{225} = 15\), the two solutions of \(x^2 = 225\) are \(15\) and \(-15\). The problem tells you \(x\) is negative, so pick that one.
review.sol: Start by finding the positive number whose square is \(225\). Since \(15^2 = 15 \times 15 = 225\), we have \(\sqrt{225} = 15\). That means \(x^2 = 225\) has two solutions, \(x = 15\) and \(x = -15\), because both \(15^2 = 225\) and \((-15)^2 = 225\). The radical symbol always points to the nonnegative root, but the equation itself keeps both. Here we are told \(x\) is negative, so we choose the negative one and get \(x = \boxed{-15}\).

review.x: Squaring any real number gives zero or a positive result, never negative. How many real values of \(x\) satisfy \(x^2 = -16\)?
hints: Try a few numbers. A positive number squared is positive, a negative number squared is also positive, and \(0^2 = 0\). Can any of these land on a negative result?
Since every real number squared gives zero or more, no real \(x\) can ever produce \(-16\). Count how many work.
review.sol: Squaring a real number can never give a negative result. A positive times a positive is positive, a negative times a negative is also positive, and \(0^2 = 0\). So \(x^2\) is always zero or larger for any real \(x\). The equation \(x^2 = -16\) asks for a real number whose square is \(-16\), which is below zero, so nothing can satisfy it. The closest perfect square nearby is \(16\), and \(\sqrt{16} = 4\) shows that \(4^2 = 16\), a positive value, never \(-16\). There are no real solutions, so the count is \(\boxed{0}\).

review.x: Squaring undoes a square root and vice versa. Evaluate \(\left(\sqrt{47}\right)^2\) without finding the decimal value of \(\sqrt{47}\).
hints: The square root of a number is the value whose square is that number. So whatever \(\sqrt{47}\) is, squaring it has to give back 47 by the very meaning of the root.
You do not need to find \(\sqrt{47}\) as a decimal. Squaring undoes the root, so \(\left(\sqrt{47}\right)^2\) is just 47 itself.
review.sol: By definition, \(\sqrt{47}\) is the nonnegative number whose square is 47. So if you take that number and square it, you must get 47 right back. You can confirm the pattern on a perfect square, where \(\sqrt{49} = 7\) and \(7^2 = 49\), and the same cancellation works for any nonnegative number, including 47. That gives \(\left(\sqrt{47}\right)^2 = 47\), so the answer is \(\boxed{47}\).

review.x: A recorded side length of \(-8\) has the wrong sign. Evaluate \(\sqrt{(-8)^2}\) to recover the nonnegative side length.
hints: Work from the inside out. First square \(-8\). A negative times a negative is positive, so \((-8)^2\) is a positive whole number.
Once you have the value under the radical, it is a perfect square. Take its square root, and remember the radical gives the nonnegative root.
review.sol: Start inside the radical and square the recorded number. Since a negative times a negative is positive, \((-8)^2 = 64\). Now the expression is \(\sqrt{64}\). The square root asks for the nonnegative number whose square is \(64\), and \(8 \times 8 = 64\), so \(\sqrt{64} = 8\). Notice what happened. Squaring turned \(-8\) into \(64\), and the radical brought back the nonnegative version, \(8\), not the original \(-8\). That is why \(\sqrt{x^2}\) gives the nonnegative size, here \(\boxed{8}\).

review.x: A square hall uses \(4{,}900\) tiles. Since \(4{,}900 = 49 \times 100\), use \(\sqrt{4{,}900} = \sqrt{49} \times \sqrt{100}\) to find the number of tiles along one edge.
hints: Break the big number into perfect-square pieces. Notice \(4{,}900 = 49 \times 100\), and you already know the roots of both of those.
Take each root on its own. \(\sqrt{49} = 7\) and \(\sqrt{100} = 10\), so the root of the product is \(7 \times 10\).
review.sol: A square root spreads across a product, so \(\sqrt{49 \times 100} = \sqrt{49} \times \sqrt{100}\). Each piece is a perfect square you can read off directly, with \(\sqrt{49} = 7\) and \(\sqrt{100} = 10\). Multiplying gives \(7 \times 10 = 70\). Check it by squaring, since \(70^2 = 4{,}900\) matches the total number of tiles, so one edge of the hall is \(70\) tiles long. \(\boxed{70}\)

review.x: A friend claims \(\sqrt{289} = 17\). Verify: does \(17^2 = 289\)? What is \(\sqrt{289}\)?
hints: A square root that comes out to a whole number is a number you can multiply by itself to land on \(289\). Try squaring a value near \(17\) and see if you hit \(289\) exactly.
Test the last-digit clue first. The root of \(289\) ends in \(3\) or \(7\). Now square \(17\) and check whether \(17^2\) equals \(289\).
review.sol: Both sanity checks point the same way, so I confirm them with a direct computation. The last-digit check says the root must end in \(3\) or \(7\), and \(17\) ends in \(7\), so it passes. The size check says squaring the candidate should land exactly on \(289\). Squaring gives \(17^2 = 17 \times 17 = 289\), which is an exact hit. A nearby wrong guess like \(19\) would fail, since \(19\) ends in \(9\) and \(19^2 = 361\) overshoots \(289\). So the root that squares back to \(289\) is \(\sqrt{289} = \boxed{17}\).

review.x: You know \(\sqrt{x} = 19\). Square both sides to find \(x\). What is \(x\)?
hints: Squaring undoes a square root, so square both sides of \(\sqrt{x} = 19\). Squaring the left side frees \(x\), and squaring the right side turns \(19\) into \(19^2\).
You need \(19^2\), which is \(19 \times 19\). Work that product out to get the whole number that \(x\) equals.
review.sol: The equation \(\sqrt{x} = 19\) keeps \(x\) trapped under the radical, and squaring is the move that undoes a square root, so square both sides. On the left, squaring the root gives back \(x\), and on the right \(19\) becomes \(19^2\), so \(x = 19^2 = 361\). To be sure, check the original equation, \(\sqrt{361} = 19\) since \(19 \times 19 = 361\), which matches. So \(x = \boxed{361}\).

review.x: A game requires solving \(\sqrt{x + 7} = -3\). The radical is always nonnegative. How many solutions exist?
hints: The radical symbol means the principal root, which is always zero or positive. Ask yourself whether any nonnegative result can equal \(-3\).
Whatever \(x + 7\) works out to, \(\sqrt{x + 7}\) is never negative, so it cannot match the negative number \(-3\). Count how many values of \(x\) can possibly work.
review.sol: The left side is \(\sqrt{x + 7}\), and the radical symbol always returns the principal root, which is zero or positive. For example \(\sqrt{16} = 4\) and \(\sqrt{49} = 7\), never a negative value. The right side is \(-3\), a negative number. A quantity that is always nonnegative can never equal a negative number, so no choice of \(x\) makes the two sides match. If you tried to solve by squaring both sides you would get \(x + 7 = 9\), which gives \(x = 2\), but checking it shows \(\sqrt{2 + 7} = \sqrt{9} = 3\), not \(-3\). That candidate fails, which confirms there is no real solution. The number of solutions is \(\boxed{0}\).

blocks.x: In 8.1 every radicand was a perfect square. Now suppose a tile setter needs a square panel covering exactly \(50\) square inches. The side is \(\sqrt{50}\), an irrational number whose decimal never ends or repeats, so we estimate it instead.

Irrational roots. When the number under the radical is not a perfect square, its root is a new kind of number. It cannot be written as a fraction, and its decimal runs forever without ever ending or settling into a repeating pattern, so there is no exact value to type. But you are not stuck, because the perfect squares from 8.1 sit at fixed, known spots on the number line. Find the two perfect squares that bracket the radicand, and you trap one of these endless roots tightly between two whole numbers you know exactly.
The Pythagoreans believed every length was a fraction. When someone proved \(\sqrt{2}\) cannot be written as any fraction, legend says the discoverer was thrown overboard for shattering that belief.
A square patch covers \(54\) sq m, so the side is \(\sqrt{54}\). Since \(7^2 = 49\) and \(8^2 = 64\), the root lies between \(7\) and \(8\). What is the largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{54}\)?
hints: Look for the two perfect squares that sandwich \(54\). Which perfect square sits just below \(54\), and which sits just above?
You found that \(49 < 54 < 64\). The roots keep that same order, so \(7 < \sqrt{54} < 8\). The floor is the smaller of those two whole numbers.
blocks.sol: The floor of \(\sqrt{54}\) is the largest whole number whose square does not pass \(54\). Compare exact squares around \(54\). The whole number \(7\) gives \(7^2 = 49\), and \(49 \le 54\), so \(7\) is safe. The next whole number \(8\) gives \(8^2 = 64\), and \(64 > 54\), so \(8\) is too big. Putting those together, \(49 \le 54 < 64\). Because a larger number always has a larger square root, that same order carries over to the roots, giving \(7 < \sqrt{54} < 8\). The side length lands strictly between \(7\) and \(8\) meters, so the largest whole number still below it is \(\boxed{7}\).

blocks.cap: The two nearest perfect squares act like fence posts. Since \(49\) sits below \(54\) and \(64\) sits above it, and a bigger number always has a bigger root, \(\sqrt{49}=7\) and \(\sqrt{64}=8\) trap \(\sqrt{54}\) inside one unit, so \(7 < \sqrt{54} < 8\) and its floor is \(7\). The peg leans toward \(7\) because \(54\) is only \(5\) above \(49\) but a full \(10\) below \(64\), so it lives in the lower part of the gap.

blocks.x: Bounding a root. A bigger number always has a bigger square root, so the moment you find the perfect square just below your number and the one just above it, you have caught the root between two neighboring whole numbers. The smaller of those whole numbers is the floor of the root, the largest whole number still below it. So \(\sqrt{54}\) is trapped between \(7\) and \(8\), since \(49\) sits just below \(54\) and \(64\) just above, which makes its floor \(7\).

Order rule. For numbers that are zero or bigger, squaring never flips the order around, so a larger number always carries a larger square root. That means \(\sqrt{a} > \sqrt{b}\) exactly when \(a > b\), and comparing two roots becomes the easy job of comparing the plain numbers hiding inside them. So \(\sqrt{53} > \sqrt{46}\) without any estimating, just because \(53 > 46\). This one rule is the engine behind trapping a root and behind everything else in the lesson.
A skylight has area \(83\) sq in. The edge \(\sqrt{83}\) is between \(9\) and \(10\). The halfway square is \(9.5^2 = 90.25\). To the nearest inch, what is \(\sqrt{83}\)?
hints: The two candidates are \(9\) and \(10\), and the halfway point between them is \(9.5\). Whichever side of \(9.5\) the root falls on is the side you round toward.

Square the halfway point instead of the root, since squaring keeps the order for nonnegative numbers. The boundary is \(9.5^2 = 90.25\). Because \(83 < 90.25\), the root is less than \(9.5\), so it rounds down.
blocks.sol: First trap the root between landmarks. Since \(9^2 = 81\) and \(10^2 = 100\), and \(81 \le 83 < 100\), the root \(\sqrt{83}\) lies between \(9\) and \(10\). To round to the nearest whole number, compare the root to the halfway point \(9.5\). Squaring keeps the order for nonnegative numbers, so instead of estimating \(\sqrt{83}\) with a long decimal, I compare \(83\) to \(9.5^2\). To stay exact, write \(9.5 = \frac{19}{2}\), so \(9.5^2 = \frac{19^2}{4} = \frac{361}{4}\). Clear the fraction by comparing \(4 \times 83 = 332\) against \(19^2 = 361\). Since \(332 < 361\), we have \(83 < 9.5^2\), which means \(\sqrt{83} < 9.5\). The root sits below the halfway mark, so it rounds down. To the nearest whole inch, \(\sqrt{83} \approx \boxed{9}\).

blocks.x: A skylight has area \(21\) sq in. The edge \(\sqrt{21}\) is between \(4\) and \(5\). The halfway square is \(4.5^2 = 20.25\). To the nearest inch, what is \(\sqrt{21}\)?
hints: The halfway point between 4 and 5 is 4.5. A root rounds up exactly when it is bigger than 4.5, which happens exactly when the number under the root is bigger than \(4.5^2\).
Compare \(21\) to \(4.5^2 = 20.25\). Since \(21\) is larger, \(\sqrt{21}\) is larger than \(4.5\), so it has already passed the halfway mark.
blocks.sol: First trap the root between whole numbers. Because \(4^2 = 16\) and \(5^2 = 25\), and \(16 < 21 < 25\), the edge \(\sqrt{21}\) lies between \(4\) and \(5\). To round to the nearest whole number, the only question is which side of the midpoint \(4.5\) it falls on. Instead of estimating a long decimal, square the midpoint and compare exactly. The midpoint squared is \(4.5^2 = 20.25\). Now compare the number under the root with that boundary. Since \(21 > 20.25\), squaring tells us \(\sqrt{21} > \sqrt{20.25} = 4.5\). The root has passed the halfway mark, so it rounds up to \(5\). The tempting guess of \(4\) was off, and the squared-boundary test settles it cleanly, giving \(\sqrt{21} \approx 5\) to the nearest whole inch, so the answer is \(\boxed{5}\).

blocks.x: Rounding a root. To round \(\sqrt{n}\) to the nearest whole number, you never have to chase its endless decimal, only find the boundary it must beat. The cutoff sits exactly halfway between the two integers the root is trapped between, so square that halfway value \(m.5\) and compare it to \(n\). If \(n > (m.5)^2\) the root rounds up, otherwise it rounds down. The whole decision is made with exact whole-number arithmetic, and no decimal for the root is ever needed.
A pendulum is \(\sqrt{23}\) ft long. The root is between \(4.7\) and \(4.8\). Does it pass the halfway square \(4.75^2 = 22.5625\)? To the nearest tenth, what is \(\sqrt{23}\)?
hints: The halfway mark between \(4.7\) and \(4.8\) is \(4.75\). Whichever side of \(4.75^2\) the number \(23\) falls on tells you which tenth the root is nearer to.

Compare exactly with whole numbers. Multiply both sides by \(10000\), so \(4.75^2\) becomes \(475^2 = 225{,}625\), while \(23\) becomes \(230{,}000\). Since \(230{,}000\) is bigger, \(23\) sits above \(4.75^2\), so the root is on the higher side.
blocks.sol: First trap \(\sqrt{23}\) between two tenths by comparing squares. Squaring \(4.7\) and \(4.8\) exactly gives \(4.7^2 = 22.09\) and \(4.8^2 = 23.04\), and since \(22.09 < 23 < 23.04\), the root lies between \(4.7\) and \(4.8\). To round to the nearest tenth, check which side of the midpoint \(4.75\) the root sits on, which means comparing \(23\) with \(4.75^2\). Square the midpoint exactly, getting \(4.75^2 = 22.5625\). To compare without trusting a long decimal, scale by \(10000\), so \(4.75^2\) becomes \(475^2 = 225{,}625\) and \(23\) becomes \(230{,}000\). Since \(225{,}625 < 230{,}000\), we have \(4.75^2 < 23\), so \(4.75 < \sqrt{23}\) and the root is past the midpoint, landing on the \(4.8\) side. Rounded to the nearest tenth, \(\sqrt{23} = \boxed{4.8}\).

blocks.x: A drone hovers at \(\sqrt{41}\) m. The root is between \(6.4\) and \(6.5\). Does it pass the halfway square \(6.45^2 = 41.6025\)? To the nearest tenth, what is \(\sqrt{41}\)?
hints: You already know the root is between \(6.4\) and \(6.5\). The only question is which tenth it rounds to, so the tiebreaker is the halfway point \(6.45\).
Compare \(41\) with \(6.45^2\). To keep it exact, square \(645\) and compare it to \(41 \times 10{,}000\). If \(41\) comes out smaller, the root is below \(6.45\) and rounds down.
blocks.sol: The number \(41\) is not a perfect square, so \(\sqrt{41}\) is irrational and we estimate it. First trap it between consecutive tenths using exact squares. Comparing without decimals, \(6.4^2 = \frac{64^2}{100} = \frac{4096}{100}\), and \(41 = \frac{4100}{100}\), so since \(4096 < 4100\) the root is above \(6.4\). Likewise \(6.5^2 = \frac{4225}{100}\), and since \(4100 < 4225\) the root is below \(6.5\). So \(\sqrt{41}\) lies between \(6.4\) and \(6.5\). To round, square the midpoint \(6.45\) and compare exactly. Squaring \(645\) gives \(645^2 = 416{,}025\), while \(41 \times 10{,}000 = 410{,}000\). Since \(410{,}000 < 416{,}025\), we have \(41 < 6.45^2\), which means \(\sqrt{41} < 6.45\). Sitting below the halfway point, the root rounds down. To the nearest tenth, \(\sqrt{41} \approx \boxed{6.4}\).

blocks.cap: Zooming in on the tenth between \(4.7\) and \(4.8\), the rounding question becomes one clean comparison. Squaring the endpoints gives \(4.7^2 = 22.09\) and \(4.8^2 = 23.04\), which traps \(\sqrt{23}\) inside this gap. The decider is the midpoint \(4.75\), whose square is \(4.75^2 = 22.5625\). Since the target \(23\) sits above \(22.5625\), the root \(\sqrt{23}\) has already crossed the halfway mark, so it rounds up to \(4.8\). Every step here is exact arithmetic on the squares, so we never have to chase the endless decimal of the root itself.

blocks.x: A square solar farm covers \(6800\) sq m. Given \(82^2 = 6724\) and \(83^2 = 6889\), what is the largest whole number of meters less than \(\sqrt{6800}\)?
hints: You want two perfect squares with \(6800\) caught between them, and you are already handed the candidates. Since \(82^2 = 6724\) and \(83^2 = 6889\), which two consecutive whole numbers do those squares belong to?
From \(6724 \le 6800 \le 6889\) you can read off \(82 < \sqrt{6800} < 83\). The largest whole number that stays below \(\sqrt{6800}\) is the smaller of those two bounds.
blocks.sol: I want the two consecutive perfect squares that bracket \(6800\), because for nonnegative numbers a bigger number always has a bigger square root. Testing the nearby whole numbers gives the exact comparison \(82^2 = 6724\) and \(83^2 = 6889\). Since \(6724 \le 6800\) and \(6800 \le 6889\), I have \(6724 \le 6800 \le 6889\). Taking square roots keeps that order, so \(82 < \sqrt{6800} < 83\). The root is strictly above \(82\) because \(6800\) is more than \(6724\), and strictly below \(83\) because \(6800\) is less than \(6889\). The largest whole number that still stays below \(\sqrt{6800}\) is therefore \(\boxed{82}\).

blocks.x: Finding the floor. To find the largest whole number below \(\sqrt{n}\), search for consecutive whole numbers \(m\) and \(m+1\) with \(m^2 \le n < (m+1)^2\), then \(m\) is the floor of the root. You can hunt for the two perfect squares that hug \(n\), or square whole numbers going up until you pass it and back off by one. It never breaks because you are only ever comparing squares, and squares stay exact even when the root runs on forever, so you never have to pin down the messy decimal at all.
How many whole numbers \(n\) satisfy \(7 < \sqrt{n} < 11\)? Square the bounds: \(n\) must satisfy \(49 < n < 121\). Count the whole numbers strictly between \(49\) and \(121\).
hints: Squaring is fair on both sides because for nonnegative numbers a bigger value always has a bigger square root, so the order never flips. From \(\sqrt{n} > 7\) you get \(n > 49\), and from \(\sqrt{n} < 11\) you get \(n < 121\).

Now \(n\) is a whole number with \(49 < n < 121\). Both ends are strict, so \(49\) and \(121\) themselves are out, since their roots are exactly \(7\) and \(11\), not between. The list runs from \(50\) up through \(120\). To count a solid run of whole numbers, take last minus first plus one.
blocks.sol: The cleanest move is to turn the root bounds into plain number bounds by squaring. That is fair because for nonnegative numbers, the larger number always has the larger square root, so squaring keeps the order intact. Start with \(\sqrt{n} > 7\). Squaring both sides gives \(n > 7^2\), which is \(n > 49\). Then \(\sqrt{n} < 11\) gives \(n < 11^2\), which is \(n < 121\). So \(n\) is a whole number with \(49 < n < 121\). Both ends are strict. The number \(49\) is excluded because \(\sqrt{49} = 7\) exactly, not greater than \(7\), and \(121\) is excluded because \(\sqrt{121} = 11\) exactly, not less than \(11\). The qualifying whole numbers run from \(50\) up through \(120\). Counting a solid run of whole numbers is last minus first plus one, so \(120 - 50 + 1 = 71\). \(\boxed{71}\)

blocks.x: Counting roots in a range. Asking how many whole numbers have a square root between two values is hard to picture while the roots are wrapped in radicals, so square both ends and turn it into a question about the plain numbers underneath. To count roots landing between \(5\) and \(6\), square to ask which whole numbers fall between \(25\) and \(36\), then count on sight. Squaring is fair because a bigger number always has a bigger root, so it never sneaks a value in or out. The only thing left to watch is whether each endpoint is counted or skipped.
How many whole numbers \(k\) satisfy \(\sqrt{130} < k < \sqrt{320}\)? The smallest valid \(k\) is \(12\) and the largest is \(17\). Count them.
hints: A whole number \(k\) fits when its square lands strictly above \(130\) and strictly below \(320\). Find the smallest such \(k\) and the largest such \(k\) by checking squares.

The smallest fit is \(12\) because \(12^2 = 144 \gt 130\) while \(11^2 = 121\) is too small. The largest fit is \(17\) because \(17^2 = 289 \lt 320\) while \(18^2 = 324\) is too big. Now count from \(12\) through \(17\), and remember to include both ends.
blocks.sol: A whole number \(k\) lands strictly between the two roots exactly when \(130 \lt k^2 \lt 320\), so instead of estimating the roots I compare the squares directly. Walking up the perfect squares, \(11^2 = 121\) is below \(130\) but \(12^2 = 144\) is above \(130\), so the smallest \(k\) that works is \(12\). Going the other way, \(17^2 = 289\) is below \(320\) but \(18^2 = 324\) is above \(320\), so the largest \(k\) that works is \(17\). The valid values are the whole numbers \(12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17\). Counting them, or computing \(17 - 12 + 1\), gives \(\boxed{6}\).

blocks.x: Whole numbers between two roots. Counting the whole numbers caught between two different roots is the trapping trick run twice. Since squaring keeps order for nonnegative numbers, asking which whole numbers \(k\) satisfy \(\sqrt{a} < k < \sqrt{b}\) is the same as asking which ones, when squared, land strictly between \(a\) and \(b\). Bracket each root by itself, read off the smallest whole number above \(\sqrt{a}\) and the largest below \(\sqrt{b}\), then count the run. The order rule keeps the count lined up so none slip past.
Cable A is \(6\sqrt{7}\) m, Cable B is \(4\sqrt{15}\) m. Square each to compare. Option 1 is Cable A, Option 2 is Cable B. Which is longer?
hints: Comparing the radicals head on is hard, but squaring clears the roots away. Square each length using \((a\sqrt{b})^2 = a^2 \times b\), which leaves you two whole numbers, then compare those.
Cable A squared is \(6^2 \times 7 = 36 \times 7 = 252\). Cable B squared is \(4^2 \times 15 = 16 \times 15 = 240\). Since both cables are positive, the one with the larger square is the longer cable.
blocks.sol: Both lengths are positive, and for nonnegative numbers a larger value always has a larger square, so squaring the two lengths lets us compare them without ever touching the roots. Squaring Cable A, \((6\sqrt{7})^2 = 6^2 \times 7 = 36 \times 7 = 252\). Squaring Cable B, \((4\sqrt{15})^2 = 4^2 \times 15 = 16 \times 15 = 240\). These are exact whole numbers, no rounding needed, and \(252 > 240\). Since squaring keeps positive numbers in the same order, the cable with the larger square is the longer one, so Cable A wins. The longer cable is option \(\boxed{1}\).

blocks.cap: Two cables, one squaring trick. Cable A is \(6\sqrt{7}\) and Cable B is \(4\sqrt{15}\), and comparing those roots head on is hard. Square each instead. Cable A becomes \(6^2 \times 7 = 36 \times 7 = 252\) and Cable B becomes \(4^2 \times 15 = 16 \times 15 = 240\). Since both cables are nonnegative, squaring keeps their order, so \(252 > 240\) tells us \(6\sqrt{7} > 4\sqrt{15}\) and Cable A wins.

blocks.x: Comparing root expressions. Two nonnegative quantities keep their order when you square them, so whichever one squares to the bigger number is the bigger quantity. To compare any two root expressions, square both and rank the plain whole numbers you get. A coefficient out front comes along for the ride, because it squares too, so \(6\sqrt{7}\) squares to \(6^2 \times 7 = 252\), turning an awkward irrational into one tidy number. The decimals you were reaching for are never needed, and this only decides which is larger, it does not simplify or combine the radicals.
Evaluate \(\left(\sqrt{6}\right)^4\). Group the four factors into two pairs. Each pair \(\sqrt{6} \times \sqrt{6} = 6\). What is the result?
hints: A square root undoes a square. Multiply two copies of the same root together, \(\sqrt{6} \times \sqrt{6}\), and you are left with just the inside, \(6\). No decimals needed.

The fourth power is two of those pairs side by side, so \(\left(\sqrt{6}\right)^4 = \left(\sqrt{6}\times\sqrt{6}\right)\times\left(\sqrt{6}\times\sqrt{6}\right) = 6 \times 6\). Now just multiply those two sixes.
blocks.sol: The trick is to never touch the decimal value of \(\sqrt{6}\) at all. Group the four copies into pairs. One pair squares the root exactly, \(\left(\sqrt{6}\right)^2 = 6\), since a square and a square root cancel for nonnegative numbers. The fourth power is just that result squared again, so \(\left(\sqrt{6}\right)^4 = \left(\left(\sqrt{6}\right)^2\right)^2 = 6^2\), and \(6^2 = 36\). You can sanity check this against the bracketing idea. We know \(2 < \sqrt{6} < 3\) because the perfect squares \(2^2 = 4\) and \(3^2 = 9\) trap \(6\) between them. Raising everything to the fourth power keeps the order, so \(2^4 < \left(\sqrt{6}\right)^4 < 3^4\), meaning the answer must land between \(16\) and \(81\). The exact value sits comfortably inside that window. So \(\left(\sqrt{6}\right)^4 = \boxed{36}\).

blocks.x: Even powers of roots. A square root and the number under it are tied together by one promise, that the root times itself gives back the inside, so \(\sqrt{a} \cdot \sqrt{a} = a\) even though \(\sqrt{a}\) by itself may never settle into a tidy decimal. Any even power of a root is just a stack of those pairs, and each pair collapses to the inside, so the irrational vanishes and a whole number is left. Thus \((\sqrt{a})^4 = a^2\) and in general \((\sqrt{a})^{2k} = a^k\), since splitting an even exponent into pairs raises the inside to half the exponent.
A hiker covers two trails: \(\sqrt{18}\) miles then \(\sqrt{33}\) miles. What is the largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{18} + \sqrt{33}\)?
hints: Bracket each root by itself first. Since \(4^2 = 16\) and \(5^2 = 25\), \(\sqrt{18}\) sits between 4 and 5. Since \(5^2 = 25\) and \(6^2 = 36\), \(\sqrt{33}\) sits between 5 and 6. That only tells you the sum is between 9 and 11, which is too wide to name a single floor.

Push the lower edge up with tenths. Check \(4.2^2 = 17.64\), which is below 18, so \(\sqrt{18} > 4.2\). Check \(5.7^2 = 32.49\), which is below 33, so \(\sqrt{33} > 5.7\). Adding the safe lower edges gives more than \(4.2 + 5.7\). Now find a matching upper edge to trap the sum between two neighbors.
blocks.sol: The total length is \(\sqrt{18} + \sqrt{33}\), and I want the largest whole number below it. Squaring is the honest tool here, since for nonnegative numbers a bigger square means a bigger root. First a rough trap. From \(4^2 = 16 < 18 < 25 = 5^2\) I get \(4 < \sqrt{18} < 5\), and from \(5^2 = 25 < 33 < 36 = 6^2\) I get \(5 < \sqrt{33} < 6\). Together that says the sum is between \(9\) and \(11\), too loose to name one floor. Now sharpen with tenths, comparing exact squares each time. Since \(4.2^2 = 17.64 < 18\), I know \(\sqrt{18} > 4.2\). Since \(5.7^2 = 32.49 < 33\), I know \(\sqrt{33} > 5.7\). Adding these safe lower edges, \(\sqrt{18} + \sqrt{33} > 4.2 + 5.7 = 9.9\), which is already above \(9\). For the ceiling, \(4.25^2 = 18.0625 > 18\) gives \(\sqrt{18} < 4.25\), and \(5.75^2 = 33.0625 > 33\) gives \(\sqrt{33} < 5.75\), so the sum is less than \(4.25 + 5.75 = 10\). The total is squeezed into \(9.9 < \sqrt{18} + \sqrt{33} < 10\), comfortably between \(9\) and \(10\). The largest whole number below it is \(\boxed{9}\).

blocks.x: A drone flies \(\sqrt{40}\) mi north then \(\sqrt{75}\) mi east. The total is between \(14\) and \(16\). Does \(\sqrt{40} + \sqrt{75}\) exceed \(15\)? What is the largest whole number below the total?
hints: The whole question is whether the sum slips below 15 or not. Rewrite the test \(\sqrt{40} + \sqrt{75} < 15\) by moving one root across, so you are asking whether \(\sqrt{40} < 15 - \sqrt{75}\). Both sides are nonnegative, since \(\sqrt{75} < 9 < 15\), so you are allowed to square both sides without flipping the comparison.

Squaring \(\sqrt{40} < 15 - \sqrt{75}\) gives \(40 < 225 - 30\sqrt{75} + 75\), which tidies up to \(30\sqrt{75} < 260\). There is still one root left, so square one more time to land on plain whole numbers. Compare \(30^2 \cdot 75\) against \(260^2\).
blocks.sol: The sum clearly beats \(14\), since \(\sqrt{40} > 6\) and \(\sqrt{75} > 8\) force \(\sqrt{40} + \sqrt{75} > 14\). The real contest is whether it stays under \(15\), so test \(15\) exactly. Ask whether \(\sqrt{40} + \sqrt{75} < 15\). Move one root to the other side, so the question becomes whether \(\sqrt{40} < 15 - \sqrt{75}\). The right side is positive because \(\sqrt{75} < 9 < 15\), so both sides are nonnegative and squaring keeps the order. Squaring gives \(40 < 225 - 30\sqrt{75} + 75\), and the right side collects to \(300 - 30\sqrt{75}\). Rearranging, \(30\sqrt{75} < 260\). One root still remains, so square once more to reach plain whole numbers. The left side squares to \(30^2 \cdot 75 = 900 \cdot 75 = 67{,}500\), and the right side squares to \(260^2 = 67{,}600\). Since \(67{,}500 < 67{,}600\), the comparison \(30\sqrt{75} < 260\) is true, every step reverses cleanly, and the sum really is below \(15\). It already cleared \(14\), so the sum sits strictly between \(14\) and \(15\). The largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{40} + \sqrt{75}\) is \(\boxed{14}\).

review.x: A square sticker covers \(34\) sq cm. Since \(5^2 = 25\) and \(6^2 = 36\), the side \(\sqrt{34}\) is between \(5\) and \(6\). What is the largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{34}\)?
hints: Look for the perfect square just below \(34\) and the one just above it. Think about which whole numbers, when squared, land on either side.
Since \(5^2 = 25\) and \(6^2 = 36\), the value \(34\) sits between them, so \(\sqrt{34}\) sits between \(5\) and \(6\). The floor is the lower of those two.
review.sol: To find the floor of \(\sqrt{34}\), hunt for the consecutive whole numbers whose squares bracket \(34\). Squaring \(5\) gives \(5^2 = 25\), which is below \(34\). Squaring \(6\) gives \(6^2 = 36\), which is above \(34\). So \(25 < 34 < 36\), and because a larger nonnegative number always has a larger square root, taking the root of each part keeps the order, giving \(5 < \sqrt{34} < 6\). The root lands strictly between \(5\) and \(6\), so the largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{34}\) is \(\boxed{5}\).

review.x: Rope 1 is \(\sqrt{63}\) m and Rope 2 is \(\sqrt{57}\) m. Both lie between \(7\) and \(8\). Use the order rule to decide: Option 1 is \(\sqrt{63}\), Option 2 is \(\sqrt{57}\). Which is longer?
hints: Both roots sit between 7 and 8, since \(7^2 = 49\) and \(8^2 = 64\). The floor is the same for both, so you cannot tell them apart that way. Look instead at what is under each root.
For nonnegative numbers, the larger number has the larger square root. Squaring \(\sqrt{63}\) gives \(63\) and squaring \(\sqrt{57}\) gives \(57\), so compare \(63\) and \(57\).
review.sol: Squaring a nonnegative number keeps the order, so to compare \(\sqrt{63}\) and \(\sqrt{57}\) I compare their squares. Squaring undoes each root, giving \(63\) and \(57\). Since \(63 > 57\), the root of \(63\) is the larger value, which means \(\sqrt{63} > \sqrt{57}\). Both roots round down to \(7\), so the floors tie, but the exact squares break the tie cleanly. Rope 1 is longer, so the answer is option \(\boxed{1}\).

review.x: A square window has area \(75\) sq in. The edge \(\sqrt{75}\) is between \(8\) and \(9\). Halfway square: \(8.5^2 = 72.25\). To the nearest inch, what is \(\sqrt{75}\)?
hints: The two whole numbers in play are 8 and 9, since 8 squared is 64 and 9 squared is 81 and 75 lands between them. Rounding just asks which one 75 is closer to, and the halfway point is 8.5.

Instead of squaring 8.5 with a decimal, double everything and compare whole numbers. The side doubled is twice the root of 75, and its square is 4 times 75, which is 300. The halfway value doubled is 17, and 17 squared is 289. Whichever square is bigger tells you which way to round.
review.sol: The side of the window is \(\sqrt{75}\), and you want it to the nearest whole inch. First trap it between landmarks. Since \(8^2 = 64\) and \(9^2 = 81\), and \(75\) sits between \(64\) and \(81\), you know \(8 < \sqrt{75} < 9\). To round, you compare \(\sqrt{75}\) to the halfway value \(8.5\). Rather than work with the decimal square \(72.25\), keep everything as whole numbers by doubling. Doubling the root gives \(2\sqrt{75}\), whose square is \(4 \times 75 = 300\). Doubling \(8.5\) gives \(17\), whose square is \(289\). Since \(300 > 289\), the root \(\sqrt{75}\) is greater than \(8.5\), so it rounds up to \(9\) inches. To the nearest whole inch, \(\sqrt{75} = \boxed{9}\).

review.x: A ramp rises \(\sqrt{44}\) feet. Since \(6^2 = 36\) and \(7^2 = 49\), the height is between \(6\) and \(7\). The halfway square is \(6.5^2 = 42.25\). To the nearest whole foot, what is \(\sqrt{44}\)?
hints: First trap the root between two integers. Find the consecutive whole numbers whose squares sit just below and just above 44, and you will know which two feet the height falls between.
Now decide which one is closer using the midpoint 6.5. Compare 44 with \(6.5^2 = 42.25\). If 44 is the larger number, the root is past the halfway point and rounds up.
review.sol: Start by trapping the root between landmark perfect squares. Because \(6^2 = 36\) and \(7^2 = 49\), and 44 sits between 36 and 49, the order rule tells us \(6 < \sqrt{44} < 7\). So the height is somewhere in the 6 to 7 range, and rounding comes down to which integer is nearer. The tie breaker is the midpoint 6.5, and squaring it gives \(6.5^2 = 42.25\). Compare the number under the root with that midpoint square. Since \(44 \ge 42.25\), the value 44 sits above the halfway mark, which means \(\sqrt{44}\) is greater than 6.5 and therefore closer to 7 than to 6. To keep the comparison fully exact you can clear the decimal by doubling before squaring, since \(\sqrt{44}\) versus \(6.5\) is the same as \(2\sqrt{44}\) versus \(13\), and squaring both nonnegative sides gives \(4 \times 44 = 176\) against \(13^2 = 169\). Because \(176 \ge 169\), the root really is past the midpoint, confirming it rounds up. To the nearest whole foot the ramp height is \(\boxed{7}\).

review.x: A wire is \(\sqrt{31}\) inches long. The root is between \(5.5\) and \(5.6\). Halfway square: \(5.55^2 = 30.8025\). To the nearest tenth, what is \(\sqrt{31}\)?
hints: You already trapped the root between \(5.5\) and \(5.6\). The only question left is which tenth it is closer to, so look at the midpoint \(5.55\).
Compare the squares instead of the roots. Since \(5.55^2 = 30.8025\) is less than \(31\), the root \(\sqrt{31}\) is bigger than \(5.55\), so it rounds up.
review.sol: Squaring keeps order for nonnegative numbers, so I can compare squares instead of roots. The consecutive tenths around the answer are \(5.5\) and \(5.6\). Their squares are \(5.5^2 = 30.25\) and \(5.6^2 = 31.36\). Since \(30.25 \le 31 \le 31.36\), the value \(\sqrt{31}\) lies between \(5.5\) and \(5.6\). To round, I test the halfway point \(5.55\), where \(5.55^2 = 30.8025\). Because \(30.8025 \le 31\), the root \(\sqrt{31}\) is greater than \(5.55\), which means it sits in the upper part of the interval and rounds up. To the nearest tenth, \(\sqrt{31} = \boxed{5.6}\).

review.x: A flagpole is \(\sqrt{88}\) units tall. The root is between \(9.3\) and \(9.4\). Halfway square: \(9.35^2 = 87.4225\). To the nearest tenth, what is \(\sqrt{88}\)?
hints: The root sits between 9.3 and 9.4 because their squares, 86.49 and 88.36, bracket 88. The tenths digit is decided by the halfway point 9.35.
Square the halfway value 9.35 to get 87.4225, then compare 88 against it. Since 88 is bigger, the root is past the halfway mark, so it rounds up.
review.sol: Start by trapping \(\sqrt{88}\) between two tenths. Squaring gives \(9.3^2 = 86.49\) and \(9.4^2 = 88.36\), and since \(86.49 \le 88 \le 88.36\), the root lies between \(9.3\) and \(9.4\). To round, compare \(88\) with the square of the midpoint \(9.35\). Working exactly, \(935^2 = 874225\) while \(88 \times 10000 = 880000\), and \(880000 > 874225\), so \(9.35^2 = 87.4225\) is less than \(88\). That means \(\sqrt{88}\) is larger than \(9.35\), past the halfway mark, so it rounds up to the next tenth. To the nearest tenth, \(\sqrt{88} \approx \boxed{9.4}\).

review.x: A square field covers \(250\) sq yd. Since \(15^2 = 225\) and \(16^2 = 256\), the side \(\sqrt{250}\) is between \(15\) and \(16\). What is the largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{250}\)?
hints: Look for the perfect square just below \(250\). Since \(15^2 = 225\), the root of \(250\) is a little more than \(15\).
Confirm the upper landmark too. Because \(16^2 = 256\) is more than \(250\), the root has not yet reached \(16\), so it is trapped between \(15\) and \(16\).
review.sol: I want the largest whole number that is still smaller than \(\sqrt{250}\), so I trap the root between two consecutive integers by comparing exact squares. The perfect square just below \(250\) is \(15^2 = 225\), and the next one up is \(16^2 = 256\). Since \(225 \le 250 \le 256\) and a larger number always has a larger square root for nonnegative numbers, I get \(15 < \sqrt{250} < 16\). The root is bigger than \(15\) (because \(250\) is more than \(225\)) but it has not reached \(16\) yet (because \(250\) is less than \(256\)). So the largest whole number below \(\sqrt{250}\) is \(\boxed{15}\).

review.x: A square reservoir has footprint \(9000\) sq m. Find the largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{9000}\).
hints: You are hunting for two consecutive whole numbers whose squares sit just below and just above \(9000\). The smaller of those two is your answer.
Try squaring numbers in the nineties. Check \(94 \times 94\) and \(95 \times 95\) and see which one slips past \(9000\).
review.sol: The largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{9000}\) is the floor of that root, so I look for the two consecutive whole numbers whose squares trap \(9000\). Testing nearby values, \(94^2 = 8836\) and \(95^2 = 9025\). Since \(8836 < 9000 < 9025\), squaring shows \(94 < \sqrt{9000} < 95\). Because squaring preserves order for nonnegative numbers, \(\sqrt{9000}\) lands strictly between \(94\) and \(95\), so the largest whole number below it is \(\boxed{94}\).

review.x: Find the largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{3{,}000{,}000}\). Since the root is in the seventeen-hundreds, test \(1{,}732^2\) and \(1{,}733^2\).
hints: You want the biggest whole number whose square is still at or below \(3{,}000{,}000\). Try a number in the seventeen-hundreds and square it, then nudge up or down.
Compare \(1{,}732^2\) against \(3{,}000{,}000\), then check \(1{,}733^2\). The answer is the larger one that does not pass \(3{,}000{,}000\).
review.sol: I want the largest whole number whose square stays at or below \(3{,}000{,}000\), because that whole number is the floor of \(\sqrt{3{,}000{,}000}\). Squaring keeps order for nonnegative numbers, so I compare exact squares instead of decimals. Testing \(1{,}732\) gives \(1{,}732^2 = 2{,}999{,}824\), which is at or below \(3{,}000{,}000\). Testing the next number up, \(1{,}733^2 = 3{,}003{,}289\), which passes \(3{,}000{,}000\). So \(1{,}732^2 \le 3{,}000{,}000 < 1{,}733^2\), which traps the root between \(1{,}732\) and \(1{,}733\). The largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{3{,}000{,}000}\) is \(\boxed{1732}\).

review.x: How many whole numbers \(n\) satisfy \(\sqrt{50} < \sqrt{n} < \sqrt{130}\)? Square the bounds and count whole numbers strictly between \(50\) and \(130\).
hints: For nonnegative numbers, a bigger number always has a bigger square root, so you can turn this into a question about \(n\) itself by squaring both ends.
Squaring \(\sqrt{50}\) gives \(50\) and squaring \(\sqrt{130}\) gives \(130\), so you need the whole numbers \(n\) strictly between \(50\) and \(130\). Count them carefully, leaving both ends out.
review.sol: The order rule from this lesson says that for nonnegative numbers, the one with the larger value has the larger square root, and the comparison runs both ways. So \(\sqrt{50} < \sqrt{n}\) is the same as \(50 < n\), and \(\sqrt{n} < \sqrt{130}\) is the same as \(n < 130\). I squared each given root to get an exact bound, with \(\left(\sqrt{50}\right)^2 = 50\) and \(\left(\sqrt{130}\right)^2 = 130\). Now I just need the whole numbers \(n\) with \(50 < n < 130\). Since both ends are left out, the smallest is \(51\) and the largest is \(129\). Counting from \(51\) through \(129\) gives \(129 - 51 + 1 = 79\) whole numbers. The count is \(\boxed{79}\).
review.label: r10

review.x: How many whole numbers \(k\) satisfy \(\sqrt{40} < k < \sqrt{210}\)? Find the smallest whole number above \(\sqrt{40}\) and the largest below \(\sqrt{210}\), then count.
hints: You do not need the exact value of either root. Find the smallest whole number whose square is bigger than 40, and the largest whole number whose square is smaller than 210.

Since \(6^2 = 36\) and \(7^2 = 49\), the root of 40 sits between 6 and 7, so the first whole number past it is 7. Since \(14^2 = 196\) and \(15^2 = 225\), the root of 210 sits between 14 and 15, so the last whole number before it is 14. Count from 7 up to 14.
review.sol: Instead of estimating the roots as decimals, compare squares, since for nonnegative numbers a bigger square means a bigger root. For the left post, \(6^2 = 36\) is less than 40 and \(7^2 = 49\) is more than 40, so \(6 < \sqrt{40} < 7\). The smallest whole number strictly above \(\sqrt{40}\) is 7. For the right post, \(14^2 = 196\) is less than 210 and \(15^2 = 225\) is more than 210, so \(14 < \sqrt{210} < 15\). The largest whole number strictly below \(\sqrt{210}\) is 14. The fenced-in whole numbers run from 7 through 14, namely 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14. Counting them gives \(14 - 7 + 1 = 8\). So \(\boxed{8}\).

review.x: Beam 1 is \(7\sqrt{5}\) m and Beam 2 is \(4\sqrt{15}\) m. Square each to compare. Option 1 is Beam 1, Option 2 is Beam 2. Which is longer?
hints: Both lengths are positive, so whichever one has the bigger square is the longer beam. Square each expression by squaring the number out front and squaring the root.
Beam 1 squared is \(7^2 \times 5\) and Beam 2 squared is \(4^2 \times 15\). Work out both whole numbers, then compare.
review.sol: Squaring a positive length keeps the order, so I can compare the squares instead of the messy roots. Beam 1 gives \(\left(7\sqrt{5}\right)^2 = 7^2 \times 5 = 49 \times 5 = 245\). Beam 2 gives \(\left(4\sqrt{15}\right)^2 = 4^2 \times 15 = 16 \times 15 = 240\). Since \(245 \ge 240\), Beam 1 has the larger square, so Beam 1 is the longer beam. The answer is option \(\boxed{1}\).

review.x: Evaluate \(\sqrt{3}^6\). Group the six factors into three pairs; each pair \(\sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{3} = 3\). What whole number is the result?
hints: Group the six copies of \(\sqrt{3}\) into pairs. Each pair \(\sqrt{3} \cdot \sqrt{3}\) is \(\sqrt{3}^2\), which equals \(3\).
Six factors make three pairs, so you are left with \(3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3\), which is \(3^3\). Work out that product.
review.sol: An even power of a root always escapes back to a whole number, because two copies of a root undo it. Write the sixth power as three squared pieces, \(\sqrt{3}^6 = \left(\sqrt{3}^2\right)^3\). Each \(\sqrt{3}^2\) is just \(3\), since squaring a square root returns the number underneath. That leaves \(3^3 = 3 \cdot 3 \cdot 3 = 27\). You can sanity check by comparing exact squares the other way, where the answer \(27\) squared is \(729\), and \(\sqrt{3}^6\) squared is \(\sqrt{3}^{12} = 3^6 = 729\) as well, so the two truly match. The irrational root vanished and a clean integer is left, \(\boxed{27}\).

review.x: Two trails are \(\sqrt{28}\) miles and \(\sqrt{52}\) miles long. What is the largest whole number less than \(\sqrt{28} + \sqrt{52}\)?
hints: Trap each root between tenths first. Try squaring 5.2 and 5.3 to pin down \(\sqrt{28}\), then square 7.2 and 7.3 to pin down \(\sqrt{52}\).
Once you have a low estimate for each root, add the two low estimates to find a number the sum is safely above, then check that the sum still stays below 13.
review.sol: I will trap each root between tenths by squaring, since for nonnegative numbers a larger square means a larger root. For the first trail, \(5.2^2 = 27.04\) which is below \(28\), and \(5.3^2 = 28.09\) which is above \(28\), so \(5.2 < \sqrt{28} < 5.3\). For the second trail, \(7.2^2 = 51.84\) which is below \(52\), and \(7.3^2 = 53.29\) which is above \(52\), so \(7.2 < \sqrt{52} < 7.3\). Adding the low ends, the total is more than \(5.2 + 7.2 = 12.4\), and adding the high ends, the total is less than \(5.3 + 7.3 = 12.6\). So the sum sits between \(12.4\) and \(12.6\), comfortably between \(12\) and \(13\). Because \(\sqrt{28} + \sqrt{52}\) is irrational it never lands exactly on a whole number, so the largest whole number below it is \(\boxed{12}\).

blocks.x: In 8.2 you estimated \(\sqrt{12} \approx 3.5\). This lesson rewrites it exactly. The number \(12 = 4 \times 3\) hides a perfect square, and \(\sqrt{ab} = \sqrt{a}\sqrt{b}\) lets you pull \(\sqrt{4} = 2\) out front, giving \(2\sqrt{3}\).
Compute \(\sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{25}\) and compare it to \(\sqrt{4 \times 25}\). Do they agree? What is \(\sqrt{100}\)?
hints: A perfect square is a number whose root is whole. Ask what whole number times itself gives \(100\).
Since \(10 \times 10 = 100\), the root of \(100\) is \(10\). Notice it matches \(\sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{25} = 2 \times 5\).
blocks.sol: Work the two sides and compare. Splitting first, \(\sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{25} = 2 \times 5 = 10\). Rooting the product, \(\sqrt{4 \times 25} = \sqrt{100} = 10\), because \(10 \times 10 = 100\). The two routes agree, which is exactly the rule we are after. A root of a product equals the product of the roots, so \(\sqrt{100} = \boxed{10}\).

blocks.x: Product rule. The square root of a product is the product of the square roots, \(\sqrt{ab} = \sqrt{a} \times \sqrt{b}\) for nonnegative \(a\) and \(b\). So a root is allowed to split anywhere its radicand factors. That is the lever for simplifying: if one of those factors is a perfect square, its root pops out as a clean whole number while the rest stays under the radical.
Simplify \(\sqrt{12}\) using \(12 = 4 \times 3\). Split into \(\sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{3}\), then root the perfect-square piece. What whole number comes out in front of \(\sqrt{3}\)?
hints: Split \(12\) as \(4 \times 3\), so \(\sqrt{12} = \sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{3}\). The \(\sqrt{3}\) part cannot be tidied, but \(\sqrt{4}\) can.
Since \(\sqrt{4} = 2\), the root becomes \(2 \times \sqrt{3}\), written \(2\sqrt{3}\). The whole number in front is \(2\).
blocks.sol: Hunt for a perfect-square factor of \(12\). Since \(12 = 4 \times 3\) and \(4\) is a perfect square, split the root, $$\sqrt{12} = \sqrt{4} \times \sqrt{3}.$$ Now \(\sqrt{4} = 2\), and \(\sqrt{3}\) has no perfect square to pull out, so it stays put. That leaves \(2 \times \sqrt{3}\), which we write \(2\sqrt{3}\). A quick check, \((2\sqrt{3})^2 = 4 \times 3 = 12\), the number we started with. The whole number in front is \(\boxed{2}\).

blocks.x: Simplifying. To simplify a square root, find the largest perfect-square factor of the radicand, take the root of that factor to pull a whole number out front, and leave whatever is left under the radical. The pulled-out number and the leftover root sit side by side, like \(\sqrt{12} = \sqrt{4}\sqrt{3} = 2\sqrt{3}\), which means \(2\) times \(\sqrt{3}\).

blocks.cap: Simplifying \(\sqrt{72}\). The number \(72\) hides the perfect square \(36\), since \(72 = 36 \times 2\). The product rule splits the root into \(\sqrt{36} \times \sqrt{2}\), and \(\sqrt{36} = 6\) steps out front while \(\sqrt{2}\) stays under the radical, giving \(6\sqrt{2}\).

blocks.x: Simplify \(\sqrt{72}\). The largest perfect square dividing \(72\) is \(36\), since \(72 = 36 \times 2\). What whole number appears in front of \(\sqrt{2}\)?
hints: Write \(72\) as \(36 \times 2\), so \(\sqrt{72} = \sqrt{36} \times \sqrt{2}\).
Since \(\sqrt{36} = 6\), the simplest form is \(6\sqrt{2}\). The number in front is \(6\).
blocks.sol: The largest perfect square dividing \(72\) is \(36\), and \(72 = 36 \times 2\). Split the root, $$\sqrt{72} = \sqrt{36} \times \sqrt{2} = 6\sqrt{2}.$$ The \(\sqrt{2}\) cannot be reduced further, so \(6\sqrt{2}\) is simplest. Check, \((6\sqrt{2})^2 = 36 \times 2 = 72\). The whole number in front is \(\boxed{6}\).

blocks.x: Simplify \(\sqrt{48}\). The largest perfect square dividing \(48\) is \(16\), since \(48 = 16 \times 3\). What number remains under the radical?
hints: Write \(48 = 16 \times 3\), so \(\sqrt{48} = \sqrt{16} \times \sqrt{3}\). Which piece stays under the root?
Since \(\sqrt{16} = 4\), the simplest form is \(4\sqrt{3}\). The number left under the radical is \(3\).
blocks.sol: Split off the largest perfect-square factor. Since \(48 = 16 \times 3\), $$\sqrt{48} = \sqrt{16} \times \sqrt{3} = 4\sqrt{3}.$$ The \(4\) comes out front and the \(3\) stays under the radical, because \(3\) has no perfect-square factor bigger than \(1\). So the number left under the radical is \(\boxed{3}\).

blocks.x: Simplify \(\sqrt{98}\). Since \(98 = 49 \times 2\), split the root. What whole number is in front of \(\sqrt{2}\)?
hints: The perfect squares to test are \(4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49\). Which one divides \(98\)? Try \(49\).
Since \(98 = 49 \times 2\), we get \(\sqrt{98} = \sqrt{49} \times \sqrt{2} = 7\sqrt{2}\). The number in front is \(7\).
blocks.sol: Search for a perfect-square factor of \(98\). Testing \(49\), we find \(98 = 49 \times 2\), so $$\sqrt{98} = \sqrt{49} \times \sqrt{2} = 7\sqrt{2}.$$ The leftover \(2\) is square-free, so \(7\sqrt{2}\) is simplest. Check, \((7\sqrt{2})^2 = 49 \times 2 = 98\). The whole number in front is \(\boxed{7}\).

blocks.x: Simplest form. A square root is in simplest form when the number left under the radical has no perfect-square factor bigger than \(1\), since at that point nothing more can be pulled out. If you pull out a small square and the leftover still hides another square, you are not finished, so reaching for the largest perfect-square factor right away does the job in a single step.
Factor \(30 = 2 \times 3 \times 5\). Every prime appears just once, so no repeated factor forms a perfect square. What is the largest perfect square dividing \(30\)?
hints: A perfect square needs a repeated factor, like \(2 \times 2\) or \(3 \times 3\). In \(30 = 2 \times 3 \times 5\), is any factor repeated?
None of \(4, 9, 16, 25\) divides \(30\). The only perfect square that does is \(1\), so \(\sqrt{30}\) is already in simplest form.
blocks.sol: Factor \(30\) completely, \(30 = 2 \times 3 \times 5\). A perfect-square factor needs a repeated prime, but here every prime shows up only once, so none of \(4\), \(9\), \(16\), or \(25\) divides \(30\). The largest perfect square dividing \(30\) is just \(1\). Since pulling out \(\sqrt{1} = 1\) changes nothing, \(\sqrt{30}\) is already in simplest form. The largest perfect-square factor is \(\boxed{1}\).

blocks.x: Simplify \(\sqrt{10} \times \sqrt{5}\). Combine under one radical: \(\sqrt{50}\). Then pull out the perfect-square factor. What whole number is in front of \(\sqrt{2}\)?
hints: Combine first, \(\sqrt{10} \times \sqrt{5} = \sqrt{50}\). Then look for the largest perfect square dividing \(50\).
Since \(50 = 25 \times 2\), we get \(\sqrt{50} = \sqrt{25} \times \sqrt{2} = 5\sqrt{2}\). The number in front is \(5\).
blocks.sol: Join the roots with the product rule, $$\sqrt{10} \times \sqrt{5} = \sqrt{10 \times 5} = \sqrt{50}.$$ Now simplify. The largest perfect square dividing \(50\) is \(25\), and \(50 = 25 \times 2\), so $$\sqrt{50} = \sqrt{25} \times \sqrt{2} = 5\sqrt{2}.$$ The whole number in front is \(\boxed{5}\).
